content.full_text:
MEMBERS
BILL FILANTE
Vice Chair
LUCILLE ROYBAL-ALLARD
Majority Vice Chair

BOB EPPLE
ROBERT FRAZEE
TRICE HARVEY
RICHARD MOUNTJOY
RICHARD POLANCO
CURTIS R. TUCKER JR

California Legislature

CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER
BOB CONNELLY

Assembly Rules Committee

COMMITTEE SECRETARY
KAYE PACKARD

ROOM 3016 - STATE CAPITOL
P.O. BOX 942849
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 94249-0001
TELEPHONE (916) 445-8173

TOM BANE
CHAIRMAN

FPPC
AUG - 4 1992

August 3, 1992


John Wallace, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95812

Dear John:

I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation this
afternoon.  We are receiving questions from Members who are
leaving at the end of the session concerning the disposition of
campaign assets such as computers, televisions, and furniture.
It is my understanding that, prior or November 30, the ordinary
personal use rules apply, and that after the session ends on
November 30, 1992, Section 89519 applies.

We are telling Members that their choices with respect to
campaign assets are:

1.  They can give them to the State Assembly, either before or
    after November 30.

2.  They can give them to a charitable, educational, civic,
    religious or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organization.
    If the gift is made before November 30, it must bear a
    reasonable relationship to a governmental, legislative, or
    political purpose.  There is no similar requirement after
    November 30.  However, either before or after November 30,
    no substantial part of the gift can have a material
    financial effect on the Member, his or her immediate
    family, the campaign treasurer, or (prior to November 30)
    anyone with authority to approve campaign expenditures.


Printed on Recycled Paper

John Wallace, Esq.
Page Two
August 3, 1992


3.  Prior to November 30, they can transfer the assets to
another candidate or committee (this would be a
contribution).  After November 30, they can only contribute
to federal or out-of-state candidates or to ballot measure
committees.

4.  Either before or after November 30, they can sell the
assets to another candidate or to a member of the public,
or they can buy the assets from the campaign for
themselves.  In any case, they should sell the assets for
their fair market value (if they sell for less than the
fair market value, they would be making either a gift or a
contribution, depending on the identity of the buyer).  The
proceeds of the sale should be put in the campaign account
and disposed of according to the personal use or surplus
funds provisions of the law.  The sale should be disclosed
on the committee's campaign statements.

There is no hard and fast rule for determining the fair market
value of used campaign assets.  The Member should use a
reasonable method, such as asking for several bids, looking at
prices charged by businesses that sell similar used goods, or
looking at newspaper advertising for private offers for sale of
similar products.  The Member should document the method used
to determine the price (particularly when the Member is buying
the asset).  If there is doubt about the fair market value in a
particular instance, they can request specific advice from the
FPPC.

Please let me know as soon as possible if you have anything to
add to this summary, so that we can get the information to the
departing members in a timely fashion.

Sincerely,

BARBARA MILMAN
Chief Counsel
Assembly Rules Committee

BM:bml

sections.question_synthetic: What are the permissible options for California state legislators to dispose of campaign assets (computers, televisions, furniture) at the end of their tenure, and what rules apply before versus after November 30, 1992?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is incoming correspondence from Assembly Rules Committee seeking FPPC confirmation rather than an FPPC advice letter response. No formal Q/A sections present. Document references prior telephone conversation and requests FPPC feedback. Classified as correspondence rather than advice_letter based on direction of communication.

embedding.summary: A letter from the California Assembly Rules Committee Chief Counsel to the FPPC confirming guidance on campaign asset disposition for departing legislators, outlining four options: gifts to the State Assembly or tax-exempt organizations, transfers to other candidates/committees, or sales at fair market value, with different rules applying before and after November 30, 1992.
